data.title: SRV: This 19% Yielder Is Far From Sustainable
data.description:
Back in October, 2024, I issued an article on NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV), which is a closed end fund focusing on the midstream space. As a midstream bull, when I noticed that the yield offered by midstream-based SRV was much higher than what we could get from the pure-play holdings, I instantly gave it a try - i.e., did a deep-dive assessment of whether the case is really a buy.

Even though I liked the underlying exposures, the unfavorable leverage structure coupled with a significant reliance on NAV sales was what held me back from deploying capital in SRV.

Since then, SRV's share price has contracted by ~11%, but on a total return basis, the CEF is up by a bit less than 10%. The ride for SRV has been quite volatile; see the chart below:

In the meantime, the midstream index has delivered both less volatile and stronger (alpha of 4%) performance compared to SRV.

However, what we have now is SRV, which is yielding almost 19%, which looks very attractive, to say the least.

Let's take a quick look at whether SRV has become a more justified buy given the elevated yield, lower base rates environment, and overall consistently strong distributions, which have not been cut since 2015, when we experienced a significant oil price plunge on a global scale.

Thesis review

First of all, we have to start with the yield aspect, which is the key value prop area that makes SRV so enticing, at least optically.

It is true that on both a TTM and FWD basis, the yield is close to 20%. For any energy- or energy infrastructure related security, this is very, very solid. In fact, I doubt that we would find a comparable CEF out there that offers this kind of income.

Yet, one of the main drivers for the elevated yield statistic is the huge special distribution, which took place right before we entered 2026 - $2.14 per unit compared to a monthly stream of $0.45 per unit:

The thing is that this is the first time SRV has made such a big payout going all the way back to 2007, when the first distribution started. Plus, the distribution was exclusively funded by the realization of long-term capital gain, not the collected income from the underlying portfolio holdings.

If we adjust for this one-off and base the yield calculus on the current monthly income streams, then we arrive at a 13.5% yield, which is still high, but nowhere close to the ~20% mark.

The next question is whether the 13.5% looks solid and sustainable enough for truly durable income investors to consider integrating it into their portfolios. The answer is not that straightforward.

There are two sides to the story.

On the one hand, SRV has deployed capital into a diversified base of energy companies (power, downstream, upstream, midstream etc.), which certainly reduces the overall risk in the Fund. For example, the AI-induced tailwinds for power utilities and energy infrastructure offset the headwinds from lower oil prices and increased political risk (pressure) on the renewables.

The Top 10 holdings consist mostly of investment grade businesses, which introduce another layer of safety.

Then SRV has sourced external leverage that currently sponsors 25.95% of the total assets. And it is positive to see that all of the borrowings here stem from short-term debt funding sources, which is exactly where a Fund wants to be given the falling base rate environment, while the long-term yields remain somewhat elevated. Namely, the lower short-term rates allow SRV to reduce the interest expense item, thereby supporting the net interest income generation, which at the end of the day is the main source for sustainable income distributions.

On the other hand, there are a couple of items, which might argue against SRV. My main concern is the type of fuel that SRV utilized for making the distributions happen. The distributions made over the past couple of months have been entirely funded by the return of capital. What it means is that a major chunk of the current income streams that we get from SRV is simply the capital that we have invested when opening a position in SRV (i.e., we are getting back our invested principal). Also, the annualized distribution of NAV greatly exceeds what SRV effectively generates on NAV - i.e., 14.05% vs. 7.07%.

The NAV dynamic chart confirms that:

Moreover, above I mentioned that the defensive portfolio allocation choice by SRV serves as a shield for energy-related risks and, in the long run, should smooth out the value profile of the Fund (the beauty of diversification).

However, this comes at the expense of the underlying core yield level. As I said, many of SRV's holdings carry robust balance sheets and have investment-grade credit ratings, which inherently tend to push down the yields (due to balanced risks). So, to fund the juicy distributions and close the gap between portfolio core yield and SRV total yield, the CEF has to rely on leverage and capital gains. The former is there, but the latter is, per definition, volatile and unpredictable. Some years it might work, and some might not.

Finally, I have to finish with the leverage part of the equation. The current interest expense that is incurred by SRV is 2.38%, which is roughly 130% below the SOFR. And this difference is just from SOFR; I am not talking about credit risk premiums, which are low but still there. So, what I am saying here is that as we go forward, unless there are additional interest rate cuts, SRV is likely to experience headwinds on the NII generation side from more expensive debt service.

The bottom line

In my humble opinion, I would say that we can forget about accessing close to 20% yield from SRV in a sustainable fashion. Also, we have to be aware that a notable part of income we get from SRV comes in the form of ROC.

Yet, does this mean that SRV is uninvestable?

The answer is it depends.

I see the merit of opening an exposure to SRV if investors want to capture truly high-yielding passive income streams in a monthly fashion, do not want to deal with K-1 forms, and seek a diversified energy position.

Personally, I am not holding any stake in SRV and instead prefer to focus on high-yielding (generally, 8%+) pure-play energy businesses that fund distributions from pure cash generation instead of capital gains harvesting or ROC.
